statement: Vue 3's reactivity system is built on ES2015 Proxy objects rather than Object.defineProperty (Vue 2). This means Vue 3 can detect property additions, deletions, and array index mutations natively — all of which required workarounds ($set, $delete, splice) in Vue 2. The Proxy wraps the entire object and intercepts get/set traps, enabling deep automatic tracking without explicit declaration of every observed key. The trade-off is that Proxy is not polyfillable, which is why Vue 3 dropped IE11 support.

Options API organizes code by option type (data, methods, computed, watch) which co-locates Vue machinery but scatters logic for a single feature across multiple sections. Composition API organizes by logical concern: all code for a feature lives together in a composable function. The philosophical shift is from 'answer Vue's structural questions' to 'write logic, then expose it to Vue'. Composition API also enables full TypeScript inference without decorators and makes logic reuse trivial (extract a useX() function) instead of relying on mixins, which had implicit dependency and namespace-collision problems.

The Single-File Component (.vue file) is Vue's fundamental unit of encapsulation, co-locating template, script, and scoped styles in one file. This is not a cosmetic choice: the SFC compiler is what enables scoped CSS (adding data-v-xxxx attributes), script setup macro transformations, and block-level TypeScript inference. The SFC boundary defines the component's identity at build time, allowing tree-shaking, hot module replacement, and tooling (Volar, vue-loader) to reason about the component holistically rather than as separate JS/CSS/HTML files that happen to be linked.

Destructuring a reactive() object loses reactivity. When you write `const { count } = reactive({ count: 0 })`, count is now a plain number — the Proxy binding is severed. The same trap applies to function parameters and return values. The fix is either (a) keep accessing via the reactive object (state.count), (b) use toRefs() to convert all properties to individual ref() wrappers before destructuring, or (c) use ref() directly instead of reactive() for values you intend to destructure. This is one of the most frequent Vue 3 migration bugs and the primary reason many teams default to ref() for everything.

ref() wraps primitive values in a { value } container so the Proxy can track mutations. Inside <script setup>, refs auto-unwrap in the template — you write {{ count }} not {{ count.value }}. But inside JavaScript (setup function body, composables, watchers) you must always use count.value. Forgetting .value produces silent bugs: the assignment mutates the raw primitive and is not tracked. A secondary trap: reactive() auto-unwraps nested refs — reading state.myRef gives the unwrapped value — which can make behavior inconsistent depending on how deeply a ref is nested. The mental model requires distinguishing template-land from JS-land at all times.

Pinia is Vue's official recommended state manager as of Vue 3, superseding Vuex. The key differences: Pinia has no mutations — state is mutated directly in actions (or even outside them for simple cases), eliminating the action→mutation ceremony Vuex required. Stores are defined as plain functions returning state/getters/actions, giving full TypeScript inference without plugin hacks. Pinia uses one store-per-feature rather than one giant tree, avoiding module namespace collisions. For Vuex 4→Pinia migrations, the mapping is: state→state, getters→getters, actions+mutations→actions. Vuex 5 was abandoned in favor of Pinia, so Vuex is effectively in maintenance mode.

Vue Router navigation guards run in a strict order: global beforeEach → per-route beforeEnter → component beforeRouteEnter. Guards must call next() (Options API) or return a value (Composition API with onBeforeRouteUpdate). The most common patterns: auth gates in global beforeEach checking a Pinia store, per-route data prefetching in beforeEnter to ensure data is ready before the component mounts, and component-level beforeRouteLeave to warn on unsaved changes. A subtle trap: beforeRouteEnter is the only guard that cannot access `this` because the component hasn't mounted yet — use the callback form of next() to get the component instance, or fetch via the store instead.

Plain Vue is a client-side SPA framework: the server sends an empty HTML shell and JS hydrates the page. Nuxt wraps Vue with a full-stack opinionated layer: file-system routing (pages/ directory), multiple rendering modes (SSR, SSG, SPA, hybrid per-route), server-side API routes (server/api/), and automatic data-fetching composables (useAsyncData, useFetch) that run on the server and hydrate on the client. The architectural choice is: choose plain Vue when you control the routing/rendering separately (e.g., embedded in a Laravel app, or an internal dashboard), choose Nuxt when you own the full stack and want SEO, TTFB, or universal data-fetching. Nuxt's trade-off is a larger abstraction surface and stricter file conventions.

<script setup> is syntactic sugar compiled by the SFC compiler to the Composition API's setup() function, but with key ergonomic differences: top-level declarations (refs, composables, imported components) are automatically available in the template without explicit return {}; defineProps, defineEmits, defineExpose are compiler macros (not runtime imports) that emit the correct component options. Limitations to know: (1) you cannot use await at the top level without wrapping in an async setup — use Suspense for async components; (2) defineProps cannot reference types defined in external files in some TS configurations (requires explicit import); (3) the component has no name unless you add a second <script> block with `export default { name: 'MyComponent' }` or use a Vite plugin like vite-plugin-vue-setup-extend.

provide/inject is Vue's native dependency injection for prop-drilling avoidance across deeply nested component trees. A parent calls provide(key, value) and any descendant (not just direct child) calls inject(key) to receive it. In Vue 3 with Composition API, provide/inject is reactive-aware: if you provide a ref or reactive object, the injected value is live. Best practices: (1) provide from app-level (app.provide) for global concerns like theme or i18n; (2) use Symbol keys to avoid naming collisions in large apps; (3) prefer readonly() wrapping of provided state to enforce single-direction data flow; (4) do not use provide/inject as a replacement for Pinia — it lacks devtools integration and is harder to serialize. The pattern is ideal for component libraries where direct prop threading breaks encapsulation.

Vue 3's watchEffect automatically tracks reactive dependencies read during its execution, re-running whenever any tracked dependency changes. In contrast, watch requires explicit source declaration and fires only when that specific source changes. The practical distinction: use watchEffect when the set of dependencies is complex and would be tedious to enumerate (e.g., watching multiple deeply nested state paths); use watch when you need the old and new values, need to trigger only on specific changes, or need lazy execution. A subtle trap: watchEffect runs immediately on mount, which can cause unintended side effects like premature API calls during SSR. Control timing with { flush: 'post' } (runs after DOM update) or { flush: 'sync' } (runs synchronously — rarely needed).

Vue Router's navigation guards are often used for authentication, but the common failure is making the auth check synchronous against a reactive store value that may not be hydrated yet (e.g., after a page refresh before the user info API returns). The guard sees an unauthenticated state and redirects to login, only for the user to be redirected back after the auth check completes — a 'flash of unauthenticated content' or redirect loop. The correct pattern is to await an auth initialization promise inside beforeEach before checking auth state, or use a dedicated authReady flag in Pinia that resolves after the initial auth check. Never redirect based on auth state if auth state may still be loading.

Pinia's $patch method is the recommended way to apply multiple state changes in a single mutation, ensuring that all watchers and devtools time-travel see the changes as a single atomic operation rather than a series of individual mutations. Two forms: $patch(object) for shallow updates (state => Object.assign), and $patch(mutation function) for complex logic including array operations. For derived state, Pinia getters are memoized based on their reactive dependencies — they re-compute only when dependencies change. A common performance mistake is implementing expensive derivations (filtering large arrays) as computed properties in components rather than as Pinia getters, causing redundant recomputation when the same data is needed by multiple components.

Vue component keys are used to force re-creation rather than reuse. The subtle failure is using a non-unique or non-stable key — such as array index — in v-for lists that undergo reordering, insertion, or deletion. When the key is the array index, Vue reuses the DOM element at position 0 for whichever item is now at position 0, potentially showing stale local state (input values, animation states) from the previous item. Always use a stable unique identifier (database ID, UUID) as the key. The opposite failure: using a key on a component to force reset when props change (a legitimate pattern for resetting form state) but forgetting that this destroys and recreates the component, triggering full lifecycle including re-fetches.

Nuxt's useAsyncData and useFetch are not just convenience wrappers — they implement the isomorphic data-fetching contract: the same fetch runs on the server during SSR, the result is serialized into the HTML payload, and the client hydrates from the serialized data without re-fetching. This is why calling fetch() directly inside setup() works in development but causes double-fetching in production (once on server, once during hydration). The key and server/client parameters control deduplication and whether a fetch runs during SSR. A common mistake is using useFetch inside a Pinia action instead of in the component setup, which breaks the deduplication key system and causes cache misses.

Vue's defineEmits in <script setup> serves a dual purpose: it declares the component's event contract for TypeScript consumers and documents the component's public interface. With TypeScript, use the type-only form: `defineEmits<{ (e: 'update:modelValue', value: string): void }>()` which gives full type inference on the parent's v-on handler. The v-model protocol in Vue 3 is: child emits update:modelValue, parent binds with v-model. For components with multiple v-model bindings (Vue 3 feature), each uses a distinct argument: v-model:firstName and v-model:lastName, corresponding to update:firstName and update:lastName emissions. This replaces Vue 2's single v-model + .sync modifier pattern.

Vue's Suspense component (experimental, ships stable in Vue 3.3+) enables async component trees to show fallback UI while awaiting async setup(). The failure mode is nesting multiple async components under a single Suspense boundary and assuming fallback shows until all are resolved — this is correct, but it means a slow component blocks the entire subtree. The fix is to use Suspense boundaries at lower granularity, wrapping each independent async section. A second failure: error handling inside Suspense requires a separate ErrorBoundary-equivalent using onErrorCaptured in a wrapper component — Suspense itself does not have an error slot in the current API.

Vite is the official build tool for Vue 3 projects, replacing Vue CLI (webpack-based). The key performance reasons: Vite serves source files as native ESM during development (no bundling step), making cold start instant and HMR updates millisecond-fast regardless of project size. For production builds, Vite uses Rollup for optimized bundling. Migration from Vue CLI: vue.config.js→vite.config.ts, process.env.VUE_APP_* → import.meta.env.VITE_*, webpack aliases → resolve.alias. Vitest integrates with Vite's config for testing. The critical production configuration: configure build.rollupOptions.output.manualChunks for code splitting (route-based or vendor splitting) to prevent a single large bundle.

Vue's Teleport built-in component renders its content at a different DOM location than where it is declared in the component tree, while keeping the component logic (reactivity, event handlers, parent-child communication) in the original component context. The primary use cases are modals, toasts, tooltips, and dropdowns that must escape parent overflow:hidden or z-index stacking contexts but should be governed by the component that triggers them. The `to` prop accepts a CSS selector string or DOM element reference. A common Teleport pitfall: Teleport content rendered to `body` during SSR creates hydration mismatches if the `body` target does not have the same content server-side; use the `:disabled` prop to disable Teleport during SSR.

Vue component testing with Vue Test Utils and Vitest follows the principle of testing behavior (what the component renders and emits) rather than implementation (internal state, method calls). The key APIs: mount() vs shallowMount() — use mount for integration-style tests that verify child component output, shallowMount for unit tests that stub all children. The wrapper.find() and wrapper.findAll() methods use CSS selectors; prefer data-testid attributes over structural selectors that break on refactoring. To test Pinia stores in component tests, use createTestingPinia() to provide an isolated store instance. For async state changes, always await wrapper.vm.$nextTick() or use flushPromises() before asserting DOM state.

Vue 3 reactive() vs ref(): reactive() returns a Proxy of an object — destructuring it loses reactivity because you extract plain values. `const { count } = reactive({ count: 0 })` — count is now a plain number, not reactive. ref() wraps a single value in an object with a .value property — it maintains reactivity through reassignment. UNSAID: the standard advice is to prefer ref() over reactive() because ref() works consistently regardless of destructuring. If you use reactive(), use toRefs() when destructuring: `const { count } = toRefs(state)`. Pinia uses reactive() internally but exposes state via storeToRefs() to enable reactive destructuring. The pattern: use ref() for primitives, ref() for objects too unless you specifically need reactive()'s nested object feel.

Vue 3 composables (equivalent to React custom hooks): a function prefixed with 'use' that uses Vue's composition API. Example: `function useCounter() { const count = ref(0); const increment = () => count.value++; return { count, increment } }`. Each composable call creates its own reactive state — state is per-component-instance. UNSAID: unlike React hooks, Vue composables don't have the 'must be called at top level' restriction — they can be called inside lifecycle hooks and conditional blocks. However, calling them outside of setup() (or the script setup block) won't automatically track reactive effects. The key advantage over mixins: explicit imports, no namespace collisions, TypeScript inference works correctly.

Vue 3 watch vs watchEffect: watchEffect automatically tracks reactive dependencies used in its callback — no explicit source needed. watch requires explicit source(s) and only runs when those change. Gotcha: watchEffect runs immediately (before mounting), which can cause issues if it accesses DOM or component refs not yet mounted. watch with `immediate: true` runs immediately; without, it only runs on source changes. UNSAID: watchEffect is convenient but can obscure which reactive values trigger the effect — making it harder to understand component behavior. watch is more explicit and predictable. For complex dependency tracking, watchEffect; for clear intent, watch. The `flush: 'post'` option runs effects after DOM updates — necessary when accessing updated DOM elements.

Vue 3 provide/inject pattern: parent provides values with `provide('key', value)`, descendants inject with `inject('key')`. Replaces deep prop drilling. For reactivity, provide a ref: `provide('theme', ref('dark'))` — injecting components react to changes. UNSAID: provide/inject breaks the explicit data flow and makes component dependencies implicit — any component in the subtree can inject without the parent knowing. This is appropriate for application-level concerns (theme, auth, locale) but wrong for component-level state sharing (use props). The recommended pattern: provide from app root or layout components, inject in leaf components. Providing computed values maintains reactivity: `provide('count', readonly(count))` — the readonly wrapper prevents injection receivers from mutating the provided value.

Pinia (Vue 3 official state management): stores defined with `defineStore('id', () => { ... })` using Composition API setup function, or `defineStore('id', { state, getters, actions })` using Options API syntax. State is reactive, getters are computed, actions are methods. UNSAID: Pinia replaces Vuex — it's lighter, has better TypeScript inference, and DevTools integration. The key Pinia advantage over Vuex: no mutations required (actions can directly mutate state), stores compose easily (one store can use another), and full TypeScript inference without complex type gymnastics. `storeToRefs(store)` extracts reactive state refs while keeping reactivity — necessary before destructuring.

Vue 3 template directives: v-if removes/adds DOM nodes (true conditionals), v-show toggles display:none (cheap toggle for frequently-switching elements). v-if has higher toggle cost (unmount/remount), v-show has higher initial cost (always renders). v-for requires :key for reconciliation — same rules as React key. UNSAID: never use v-if and v-for on the same element — v-for has higher priority in Vue 2, v-if has higher priority in Vue 3. The fix: wrap the v-for element with a template (virtual wrapper) and put v-if on the template. v-else and v-else-if must immediately follow a v-if or v-else-if element. v-memo (Vue 3.2+) memoizes a subtree, skipping re-render if the memo array values are unchanged — similar to React.memo.

Vue Router 4 (Vue 3): declarative `<RouterLink>`, programmatic `router.push({ name: 'route', params: { id } })`. Nested routes with `children` array, `<RouterView>` renders the matched component. Navigation guards: `router.beforeEach((to, from) => { ... })` for auth checks. UNSAID: using named routes (`router.push({ name: 'home' })`) instead of path strings prevents broken navigation when paths change. Route-level code splitting: `component: () => import('./views/Home.vue')` — dynamic import enables per-route bundles. The `meta` field on routes carries custom data (auth requirements, page titles) accessible in navigation guards. Vue Router 4's composition API: `useRoute()` and `useRouter()` replace `this.$route` and `this.$router`.

Vue 3 component emit validation: `defineEmits(['update:modelValue', 'close'])` declares emittable events but doesn't validate payload types. For TypeScript validation: `defineEmits<{ 'update:modelValue': [value: string]; close: [] }>()`. UNSAID: undeclared emits still work (Vue doesn't block them) but generate warnings and break IDE autocomplete for parent components. The v-model pattern on custom components: component emits `update:modelValue`, parent uses `v-model`. Vue 3 allows multiple v-model bindings: `v-model:title` and `v-model:content` on the same component, emitting `update:title` and `update:content` respectively — eliminating the need for separate props+emits for multi-field form components.

Vue 3 TypeScript with script setup: `<script setup lang='ts'>` enables Composition API with TypeScript. Props typing: `defineProps<{ title: string; count?: number }>()`. Default values: `withDefaults(defineProps<Props>(), { count: 0 })`. Emit typing: `defineEmits<{ submit: [value: string] }>()`. UNSAID: Vue 3.3+ supports generic components: `<script setup lang='ts' generic='T extends string'>` — the generic T is available in the component's props and template. The Volar IDE extension (formerly Vue Language Tools) provides TypeScript support in .vue files — without it, TypeScript checking in templates doesn't work. Vue 3 + TypeScript component props are slightly more verbose than React (defineProps + withDefaults) but provide the same compile-time safety.

Vue 3 reactivity pitfalls with arrays: while Vue 3 Proxy handles array index mutations (`arr[0] = value`) correctly, there are subtle issues with reactive arrays and Vue's rendering. Using non-reactive arrays in reactive() objects is fine. UNSAID: the Vue 2 array mutation method wrappers (push, pop, splice, sort, reverse, shift, unshift) were needed because Vue 2 couldn't detect array mutations. In Vue 3, all array mutations are detected by Proxy. The remaining gotcha: replacing an array entirely `this.items = newItems` in Options API must use the component's reactive reference — reassigning a local variable doesn't work. In Composition API, `items.value = newItems` (for ref) or `state.items = newItems` (for reactive object property) both work correctly.

Vue 3 Teleport component: renders content to a different DOM node while keeping it logically inside the component tree. `<Teleport to='body'><Modal /></Teleport>` renders the modal at the document body, fixing z-index and overflow issues. UNSAID: Teleport maintains the reactive connection — props and events flow through the component tree as if the modal were in its original position. Unlike React portals, Vue Teleport has first-class support via template syntax. The `disabled` prop conditionally enables/disables teleporting. Teleport into `disabled` state renders content inline. Multiple teleports to the same target stack in document order — useful for layered UI like overlay stacks.

Vue 3 Suspense for async components: `<Suspense>` with default slot (async component) and fallback slot (loading state). Works with defineAsyncComponent and async setup() functions. `<Suspense><template #default><AsyncComponent /></template><template #fallback><Spinner /></template></Suspense>`. UNSAID: Vue 3 Suspense is still experimental API — it may change. Async setup() function (returning a Promise) causes the component to suspend until the promise resolves. This enables data fetching inside setup() with `await fetch(url)` — no explicit loading state needed. The parent Suspense catches the suspension and shows the fallback. Nested Suspense boundaries each handle their own loading state independently.

Vue 3 custom directive lifecycle hooks differ from Vue 2: `mounted` replaces `inserted`, `updated` replaces `update`, `beforeUnmount` replaces `unbind`. Directives have hooks: created, beforeMount, mounted, beforeUpdate, updated, beforeUnmount, unmounted. UNSAID: custom directives in Vue 3 should be used sparingly — they manipulate the DOM directly, bypassing Vue's reactivity system. The appropriate use cases: DOM-focused libraries (focus management, resize observers, intersection observers, third-party plugins). Component-level logic belongs in composables, not directives. The binding parameter in directive hooks contains: value (current value), oldValue (previous value), arg (directive argument), modifiers (object of modifiers).

Vue 3 transition and animation: `<Transition>` wraps a single element/component, applying enter/leave CSS classes. `<TransitionGroup>` handles lists with enter/leave/move transitions. CSS class convention: v-enter-from, v-enter-active, v-enter-to (and -leave- equivalents). Named transitions: `<Transition name='fade'>` applies fade-enter-from, etc. UNSAID: the `-active` class is applied throughout the entire transition duration — put `transition: all 0.3s` here. `-from` is the starting state, `-to` is the ending state — these are snapshot classes added briefly. TransitionGroup's `move` transition (v-move class) enables smooth list reordering with FLIP animation technique. Combining with Pinia/reactive state changes: wrap the data update and Vue schedules the transitions automatically.

Vue 3 defineCustomElement: converts a Vue SFC into a Web Component (Custom Element). `const MyElement = defineCustomElement({ props: { greeting: String }, template: '

{{ greeting }}

' })`. `customElements.define('my-element', MyElement)`. Enables Vue components to be used in any HTML page, React app, or Angular app as standard HTML elements. UNSAID: Vue's reactivity system works inside the custom element — props changes trigger re-renders. Scoped styles are encapsulated via Shadow DOM. The limitation: Vue's teleport, provide/inject, and app-level plugins don't cross the custom element boundary. Custom elements are appropriate for micro-frontend scenarios or embedding Vue widgets in non-Vue applications.

VitePress for Vue-based documentation sites: static site generator using Vue 3 components and Vite. Markdown files with YAML front matter, Vue components in markdown, theming via CSS custom properties. Default theme includes: sidebar navigation, search (local or Algolia), mobile responsive layout. UNSAID: VitePress is the standard for Vue ecosystem documentation — Vue 3 docs, Vite docs, Pinia docs all use VitePress. Configuration in `docs/.vitepress/config.ts` defines navigation, sidebar, and theme options. Custom Vue components can be imported in Markdown: `<Counter />` renders the Vue component inline. The build produces fully static HTML — no JavaScript required for content rendering, only for interactivity.

Vue 3 reactivity with class instances: reactive() and ref() work with plain objects (POJOs) but have limitations with class instances — methods accessing `this` may not be tracked correctly, and class-based inheritance can confuse the Proxy wrapper. UNSAID: the Vue 3 docs recommend using plain objects for reactive state rather than class instances. If you must use classes, ensure methods don't bypass the Proxy (don't store class instance in a plain variable, always access through the reactive wrapper). The ORM-style pattern (Vuex models with class methods) that was popular in Vue 2 is generally replaced with Pinia stores + plain TypeScript interfaces in Vue 3.

Vue 3 v-model modifiers: built-in (.lazy, .number, .trim) and custom modifiers. Custom modifiers are passed to defineProps as `modelModifiers`. Pattern: `v-model.capitalize='text'` — in the component, check `props.modelModifiers.capitalize` and apply transformation in the emit. UNSAID: .number modifier uses parseFloat() (not parseInt) — input '1.5' becomes 1.5 number. .lazy delays sync to change event (fires on blur) instead of input event — appropriate for form fields where you want validation on completion, not on each keystroke. Custom modifiers enable building composable input behaviors directly into the component's v-model interface without separate props.

VueUse library (Collection of Vue Composition Utilities): 200+ composables for browser APIs (useMouse, useScroll, useIntersectionObserver), sensors, network (useFetch, useEventSource), state (useStorage, useRefHistory), utilities (useDebounce, useThrottle). UNSAID: VueUse is the Vue equivalent of React's @uidotdev/usehooks or the custom hook ecosystems. Before writing a custom composable, check VueUse — it likely already exists. VueUse composables are SSR-safe, TypeScript-first, and tree-shakeable. The `useStorage` composable persists reactive state to localStorage/sessionStorage with JSON serialization and reactivity — equivalent to React's zustand-persist in a single composable.

Vue 3 SSR with Nuxt 3: server-side rendered components have access to Nuxt's server context (event, cookies, headers) via useNuxtApp(). Client-side components use `if (process.client)` guard or `<ClientOnly>` wrapper. Hydration mismatches cause SSR errors — same rules as React hydration. UNSAID: Nuxt 3's SSR rendering pipeline is fully asynchronous — setup() can await data fetching directly. The `useFetch`, `useAsyncData`, and `$fetch` composables are Nuxt-specific and handle SSR deduplication (fetch on server, no refetch on client after hydration). Using regular fetch() in setup() without Nuxt's composables causes double-fetching: once on server, once on client after hydration. Nuxt's auto-imports eliminate manual imports for Vue and Nuxt composables — a significant DX improvement.

Vue 3 render functions and JSX: `h()` function creates virtual DOM nodes. JSX support via @vitejs/plugin-vue-jsx. When to use render functions: programmatic component generation, complex dynamic component logic, component libraries that need maximum flexibility. UNSAID: Vue's template compiler compiles templates to render functions at build time — templates are syntactic sugar for h() calls. Using render functions directly gives the same performance as compiled templates. JSX in Vue is closer to React's JSX but uses Vue event naming: `onClick` (camelCase) for Vue JSX vs `onClick` for React JSX — they're the same. The key difference: Vue JSX uses `v-model:xxx` props while React uses controlled component patterns manually.

Vue 3 template ref limitations: `<input ref='inputRef'>` populates `inputRef` with the DOM element after mounting. Accessing `inputRef.value` before mounting (in setup(), before onMounted) returns null — the DOM doesn't exist yet. Template refs on components return the component's public interface (defineExpose()). UNSAID: Vue 3 components by default expose nothing — parent refs to components see an empty object unless the component uses defineExpose({ focus, clear, ... }) to explicitly expose methods. This is the Vue 3 breaking change from Vue 2 where all component methods were accessible via $ref. The principle: components control their own API surface via defineExpose. Template ref in v-for: each iteration populates the ref array at the same index as the v-for item.

Vue 3 dynamic components: `<component :is='currentComponent'>` renders the component stored in the variable. With KeepAlive: `<KeepAlive><component :is='tab' /></KeepAlive>` caches the component instance instead of unmounting it on tab switch. UNSAID: KeepAlive's `include` and `exclude` props (string, regex, or array) control which components are cached. The `max` prop sets the maximum cached instances (LRU eviction for oldest). KeepAlive adds onActivated and onDeactivated lifecycle hooks to the wrapped component — use these instead of onMounted/onUnmounted for effects that should run on visibility changes. The cached component is NOT destroyed — its state (form inputs, scroll position, WebSocket connections) persists across tab switches.

Vue 3 computed property with side effects is an anti-pattern: computed properties should be pure transformations of reactive state with no side effects (no API calls, no DOM mutations, no state writes). Side effects in computed cause: (1) unpredictable execution timing, (2) infinite reactivity loops (computed reads A, writes B, B affects A), (3) multiple executions per render. UNSAID: the fix is always to move side effects to watch or watchEffect. Computed properties are lazily evaluated (only when dependencies change AND the value is read), which makes side effects even less predictable. The performance optimization: computed values are cached between renders and only recomputed when dependencies change — this cache is broken by side effects.

Vue 3 project setup with Vite (2024 standard): `npm create vue@latest` — official Vue project scaffolder (create-vue). Includes: Vite, Vue Router, Pinia, ESLint, Prettier, Vitest, Playwright options. The scaffolded project includes Volar IDE setup instructions. UNSAID: `npm create vue@latest` replaced the old `vue create` (Vue CLI) which used webpack. Vue CLI is no longer maintained. The Volar extension replaces Vetur for Vue 3 TypeScript support — running both causes conflicts. Volar v2 (Vue Language Tools) splits Volar into separate extensions for Vue and TypeScript. The `vue-tsc` CLI tool checks TypeScript types in .vue files in CI pipelines.

Vue 3 slot patterns: default slot, named slots, scoped slots. Named slots: `<template #header>...</template>` in parent, `<slot name='header'>` in child. Scoped slots: child passes data up to parent's slot template — enables headless component pattern. `<DataTable :data='users'><template #row='{ item }'>{{ item.name }}</template></DataTable>`. UNSAID: scoped slots are Vue's answer to React's render props pattern — the parent controls rendering while the child controls data and behavior. This enables headless components in Vue: the component manages state and behavior, the parent controls the visual output. Renderless components (returning nothing from setup, only scoped slots) are the Vue equivalent of React's custom hooks returning state and handlers.

Vue 3 Vapor mode (experimental): non-virtual DOM rendering for maximum performance. Components in Vapor mode compile to direct DOM operations without the virtual DOM diffing. Opt-in per component with `vapor` compiler option. Expected to ship as stable in Vue 3.x. UNSAID: Vapor mode is inspired by Solid.js's fine-grained reactivity model — reactive effects directly update DOM nodes without full component re-rendering. The benchmark difference: Vapor mode components perform comparably to Solid.js, significantly faster than standard Vue 3 virtual DOM. The trade-off: Vapor components can't be used alongside non-Vapor components in the same subtree (they're different rendering models). For most applications, standard Vue 3 performance is sufficient — Vapor is for performance-critical scenarios.

Vue 3 + Nuxt UI library ecosystem: (1) shadcn-vue (shadcn/ui port for Vue), (2) PrimeVue (comprehensive component library with multiple themes), (3) Naive UI (modern, TypeScript-first), (4) Headless UI for Vue (@headlessui/vue, from Tailwind Labs). UNSAID: shadcn-vue provides the same copy-paste ownership model as React's shadcn/ui — components are code you own, not npm dependencies. PrimeVue is the most comprehensive with 90+ components including DataTable, Charts, and Calendar. Naive UI is notable for 100% TypeScript coverage and type-safe theming. Vuetify 3 (Material Design) is the most popular Vue component library overall — extensive ecosystem but opinionated Material Design aesthetics.

Vue 3 reactivity loss from Object.assign: `Object.assign(state, newData)` on a reactive object works correctly — Object.assign mutates the target object, and the Proxy intercepts the property assignments. BUT: `Object.assign({}, state, newData)` creates a plain object (loses reactivity). UNSAID: the subtle reactive-vs-non-reactive issue: Proxy traps only fire when accessing through the proxy. Creating a new plain object with spread or Object.assign bypasses all traps. The spread of a reactive object `{ ...reactiveObj }` is safe for creating a snapshot (non-reactive) but incorrect for maintaining reactivity. In Composition API, `const state = reactive({...})` then `state.field = value` correctly triggers updates. Pinia handles this correctly internally — store state modifications go through the store, not direct object manipulation.

Vue 3 testing with Vitest + Vue Test Utils: `mount(Component, { props, slots, global: { plugins: [pinia, router] } })` renders the component. Assertions: `wrapper.text()`, `wrapper.find('button')`, `wrapper.trigger('click')`. Pinia testing: `createTestingPinia({ initialState })` creates an isolated store for testing. UNSAID: Vue Test Utils v2 is the official Vue 3 testing library. The `global` mount option replaces `localVue` from Vue Test Utils v1. `shallowMount` stubs child components — useful for isolation but can hide integration bugs. `flushPromises()` from @vue/test-utils waits for all pending promises to settle — required for async operations in tests. Testing composables: call them inside a mounted component or use the test-utils helper `withSetup()`.

Vue 3 lifecycle hooks in Composition API: onMounted, onBeforeUnmount, onUpdated, etc. A key difference from Options API: multiple onMounted calls in the same setup() are all called (they stack), not overridden. UNSAID: this is intentional for composables — each composable can register its own lifecycle hooks without conflicting. The composable pattern: `useFoo()` calls onMounted and onBeforeUnmount internally — the caller doesn't need to know about the lifecycle management. Vue 3 also adds onServerPrefetch (SSR only, await data before rendering on server) and onActivated/onDeactivated (KeepAlive). The setup() function itself runs before onBeforeMount — it's the equivalent of the constructor/created combination from Options API.

Vue 3 global properties and app-level configuration: `app.config.globalProperties.$http = axios` — adds $http to all components. `app.provide('apiUrl', url)` — provides at app level, available to all components via inject(). `app.directive('focus', ...)` — registers global directives. UNSAID: global properties are the Vue 3 replacement for Vue.prototype (Vue 2) — but should be used sparingly. The idiomatic Vue 3 approach for shared utilities is composables (imported explicitly per component) rather than global properties. Global properties are appropriate for: plugins that need global access (vue-router's $router, vuex's $store), and legacy Vue 2 APIs that can't be easily migrated.

Vue 3 to Nuxt 3 migration: Nuxt 3 is Vue 3 with file-based routing, auto-imports, SSR, and server API routes. Migration steps: (1) Replace vue-router manual routes with pages/ directory structure, (2) Replace Pinia initialization with Nuxt's usePinia, (3) Replace manual fetch with useFetch/useAsyncData, (4) Add server/ directory for API routes (replaces separate backend). UNSAID: Nuxt 3's auto-imports eliminate explicit imports for Vue composition API and Nuxt composables — no `import { ref, computed } from 'vue'` needed. This DX improvement reduces boilerplate significantly. The pages/ directory filename conventions mirror Next.js: [id].vue for dynamic routes, [...slug].vue for catch-all routes. Nuxt DevTools provides visual inspection of pages, components, composables, and server routes.

Vue 3's template syntax is HTML-compatible: valid HTML attributes, no JSX transformation required. This means Vue templates can be written in .html files, processed by any HTML editor, and incrementally adopted in server-rendered applications by adding Vue to individual pages. UNSAID: this design choice is Vue's competitive advantage for developers coming from traditional server-rendered applications (PHP, Rails, Django). React's JSX requires a build step; Vue's template syntax works with progressive enhancement. The trade-off: Vue templates have less expressive power than JSX (you can't write arbitrary JavaScript expressions, only supported directives). For full JavaScript expressiveness, Vue also supports render functions.

Vue 3 Pinia with SSR: Pinia stores are singletons in SPA mode but must be per-request in SSR mode to prevent state leakage between users. Nuxt 3 handles this automatically via the pinia plugin. In custom SSR setups, create a new Pinia instance per request: `const pinia = createPinia()` inside the request handler. UNSAID: this is the same SSR state leakage problem as React's module-level state. Server-side singleton stores leak user A's data to user B in concurrent requests. Nuxt 3's SSR-safe Pinia: `useStore()` returns the store scoped to the current SSR context (via vue app injection). The `$pinia` symbol on the Vue app ensures each request gets its own store instances. Testing SSR behavior: verify stores reset between requests with concurrent test scenarios.

Vue 3 form validation with Vee-Validate + Zod: `useField()` and `useForm()` from vee-validate with Zod schema validation via `toTypedSchema()`. Pattern: define Zod schema, wrap with toTypedSchema, pass to useForm, use Field components or useField for each input. UNSAID: Vee-Validate is the Vue equivalent of React Hook Form — uncontrolled inputs for performance. The `<Field>` component provides a slot-based API for custom input rendering. Schema validation with Zod/Yup/Joi all supported via adapter packages. The distinction between vee-validate and vue3-form-kit: vee-validate is lower level (validation only), vue3-form-kit provides complete form UI. For server-side validation errors, vee-validate's `setFieldError()` and `setErrors()` programmatically set validation errors from API responses.

Vue 3 nextTick usage: `await nextTick()` waits for Vue to complete DOM updates after a reactive state change. Required when you need to read DOM values (dimensions, positions) after state changes. UNSAID: the common mistake is reading DOM dimensions in the same tick as state changes — the DOM hasn't updated yet. Pattern: `count.value = 10; await nextTick(); const height = element.value.offsetHeight`. In tests with Vue Test Utils, `await nextTick()` waits for reactive updates to complete before assertions. Multiple nextTick calls are sometimes needed for deeply nested reactive updates. nextTick returns a Promise — always use `await nextTick()` for reliable timing.

Vue 3 defineModel macro (Vue 3.4+): simplified v-model implementation. `const model = defineModel<string>()` replaces the manual `defineProps({ modelValue })` + `defineEmits(['update:modelValue'])` pattern. Usage in template: `<input v-model='model'>`. UNSAID: defineModel is a compile-time macro that generates the correct props and emits automatically. The generated code is identical to the manual pattern but much less boilerplate. For named v-model: `const title = defineModel<string>('title')`. Modifiers are supported: `const [model, modifiers] = defineModel()` — the second destructured value is the modifiers object. This is a significant Vue 3.4 DX improvement — reduces v-model component implementation from 6 lines to 1.

Vue 3 error handling: `app.config.errorHandler = (err, instance, info) => { ... }` global error handler. Component-level: `onErrorCaptured((err, instance, info) => { ... })` — propagation stops if the hook returns false. Async errors in setup() and async event handlers are captured. UNSAID: Vue's error boundary equivalent is a component using onErrorCaptured — returning false stops the error from propagating to parent components. Unlike React's class-based ErrorBoundary, Vue's onErrorCaptured works in any component with Composition API. The global errorHandler is the catch-all for errors that escape component boundaries. Integration with error monitoring: `app.config.errorHandler = (err) => Sentry.captureException(err)`.

Nuxt 3 server-side API routes: `server/api/users.ts` creates a `/api/users` endpoint. Handler exports `defineEventHandler(async (event) => { ... })`. Access request body with `readBody(event)`, query params with `getQuery(event)`, cookies with `getCookie(event, 'name')`. UNSAID: Nuxt's server API routes run on the server (not client) — ideal for database queries, secret API calls, and proxying third-party APIs without exposing keys. The `$fetch('/api/users')` client-side call goes through the network; calling the same function in a server component or server route reuses the request context without HTTP overhead. Nuxt's `useNitro()` provides access to the Nitro server instance. Server routes support all HTTP methods via `defineLazyEventHandler` and method-specific files (users.post.ts, users.get.ts).

Vue 3 performance anti-pattern: using v-for with expensive computed properties inside the loop. Each list item's computed runs for every render. UNSAID: move expensive computations outside the v-for loop when possible. For item-specific computations, memoize with a Map keyed by item ID rather than computing in the template. v-memo inside v-for can memoize individual items: `<div v-for='item in list' v-memo='[item.id]'>` — skips re-rendering each row unless item.id changes. This is more granular than v-memo on the parent list. Performance-sensitive lists (100+ items with complex rendering) benefit from: v-memo for partial memoization, virtual scrolling for large datasets, and careful computed property design.

Vue 3 component communication patterns summary: (1) props down, emits up (parent-child), (2) provide/inject (ancestor-descendant skip levels), (3) Pinia store (any-to-any), (4) event bus via mitt library (decoupled event broadcasting), (5) composables with shared state (module-level reactive state). UNSAID: mitt is the lightweight Vue 3 event bus (9 bytes) — replaces Vue 2's built-in event bus (`new Vue()` as event bus). For complex apps, prefer Pinia over event bus — event buses have no visibility into who's listening and no DevTools support. The anti-pattern: deep event bus usage that obscures data flow. The legitimate use case: cross-component events that don't share state, like analytics events or keyboard shortcuts.

Vue 3 DevTools debugging: Vue DevTools browser extension shows component tree, reactive state, Pinia stores, and performance timeline. Key workflows: (1) Select component in tree to inspect state, (2) Modify state directly in DevTools for testing, (3) Pinia store tab shows all store instances and state, (4) Timeline tab records mutations with timestamps. UNSAID: Vue DevTools Vite plugin (`vite-plugin-vue-devtools`) provides browser-embedded DevTools for Vite projects — no extension install needed. Component highlighting (hover in DevTools = highlight in browser) accelerates component identification. The 'Inspector' tab shows computed properties and their dependencies — invaluable for debugging why a computed isn't updating.

Vue 3 TypeScript strict template checking: by default, Vue templates have loose TypeScript checking. Enabling strict mode in tsconfig.json + volar.config.ts increases strictness. Template expression errors are caught at build time with `vue-tsc`. UNSAID: the most common TypeScript-template mismatch: using a possibly-undefined property in a template without null check — passes TypeScript in the script block but not in the template with strict mode. Enabling strict template type checking catches these at build time instead of runtime. The `<template lang='ts'>` is not required for TypeScript — the script block's types flow into the template automatically via Volar. The `defineComponent()` wrapper provides TypeScript inference in IDEs without Volar for legacy setups.

Vue 3 internationalization with vue-i18n v9: `useI18n()` composable provides `t()` function, `locale` ref, `d()` for date formatting, `n()` for number formatting. Type-safe translations: generate TypeScript types from translation files. UNSAID: vue-i18n's Composition API mode (`legacy: false`) is required for Vue 3 Composition API integration. The Options API mode (legacy: true) is Vue 2 compatible but not recommended for new projects. vue-i18n DevTools integration shows translation keys with missing translations highlighted. Lazy-loading namespaces: `loadLocaleMessages('en', () => import('./locales/en.json'))` defers loading until needed. The `@intlify/unplugin-vue-i18n` Vite plugin enables build-time message pre-compilation — faster runtime parsing.

Nuxt 3 data fetching comparison: `useFetch(url)` for URL-based fetching with automatic SSR deduplication; `useAsyncData(key, fn)` for custom async functions; `$fetch(url)` for imperative fetching in event handlers or server routes. UNSAID: useFetch wraps useAsyncData + $fetch internally. The unique key parameter in useAsyncData ensures each data fetch has a unique cache key — duplicate keys reuse cached data. The `refresh()` and `clear()` functions from useFetch enable manual refetching and cache clearing. The `lazy: true` option defers fetching until after navigation (client-side only) — used for non-critical data that doesn't block page navigation. Server-side fetch errors are serialized and replayed on the client, making error handling seamless across SSR/hydration.

Vue 3 reactive state mutation in child components: mutating a prop directly (`props.items.push(item)`) bypasses Vue's reactivity system in the parent — the parent doesn't know the mutation happened. Props should be treated as read-only in child components. UNSAID: Vue 3 adds a runtime warning for direct prop mutation. The fix: emit an event with the new value (`emit('update:items', [...props.items, item])`) or use v-model for two-way binding. For deeply nested objects passed as props, the mutation warning may not trigger (Vue can't easily detect deep mutations through props without reactive wrapping) — this is a silent bug pattern. The safest pattern: treat all props as read-only, always use emits for parent communication.

Vue 3 async component pattern with defineAsyncComponent: `defineAsyncComponent(() => import('./HeavyComponent.vue'))`. Options: loading component (shown while loading), error component (shown on failure), delay (ms before showing loading), timeout (ms before showing error). UNSAID: defineAsyncComponent + Suspense is the equivalent of React.lazy + Suspense. Without Suspense, defineAsyncComponent uses its built-in loading/error components. With Suspense, the Suspense boundary's fallback slot is shown instead. The delay option (default 200ms) prevents loading flicker for fast connections — don't show a spinner if the component loads in under 200ms. Combining with route-level code splitting: put heavy components in separate chunks using dynamic import.

Vue 3 accessibility implementation: use semantic HTML (button not div), ARIA attributes for dynamic content, keyboard navigation for custom components. vue-a11y library provides composables for common patterns (useFocusTrap, useFocusVisible). eslint-plugin-vuejs-accessibility catches template-level violations. UNSAID: ARIA live regions for dynamic content updates: `aria-live='polite'` announces changes to screen readers without interrupting. Custom components replacing native browser controls (custom select, datepicker) must fully reimplement keyboard interactions (Arrow keys, Enter, Escape, Home/End). @headlessui/vue provides accessible headless primitives — the same accessibility that Radix provides for React. Testing: use axe-core in Vitest tests (`axe(wrapper.element)`) for automated accessibility checks.

Vue 3's SFC compilation produces both the component definition AND the template render function. This build-time compilation is what makes Vue templates as performant as hand-written render functions — no runtime template parsing. The compiler also optimizes templates: static content is hoisted out of the render function (renders once, never re-renders), dynamic patches are identified with shape flags for efficient diffing. UNSAID: Vue 3's compiler optimizations (static hoisting, patch flags, block trees) make it faster than React in many benchmarks because React's Virtual DOM diffs the entire component tree while Vue's block tree algorithm only diffs the dynamic parts. The developer experience is declarative templates; the runtime performance is efficient targeted updates.

Vue 3 shallowRef vs ref: shallowRef wraps the value in a ref but does NOT deep-convert the value to reactive. Mutations to the inner object properties don't trigger updates — only replacing the .value reference triggers reactivity. UNSAID: use shallowRef for large data structures (arrays of hundreds of objects) where deep reactivity is unnecessary — it avoids the Proxy overhead for every nested property. The failure mode: `shallowRef({ count: 0 })` then `obj.value.count++` — no reactive update triggered. You must do `obj.value = { ...obj.value, count: obj.value.count + 1 }` (replace the reference) to trigger an update. shallowReactive() similarly creates a reactive object where only the top-level properties are reactive.

Vue 3 + TanStack Query: @tanstack/vue-query provides the same query management as React Query but with Vue reactivity. `useQuery({ queryKey: ['todos'], queryFn: fetchTodos })` returns reactive `{ data, isLoading, error }`. `useMutation` for writes. UNSAID: TanStack Query is framework-agnostic at its core — the Vue adapter uses Vue refs for reactive query results instead of React state. This means the same mental model (staleTime, cacheTime, invalidation) applies to both. VueQuery DevTools extension available for cache inspection. For Nuxt 3, @tanstack/vue-query SSR support enables server-side prefetching with dehydration/hydration — matching Next.js + TanStack Query's SSR pattern.

Vue 3 component instance vs reactive state: `getCurrentInstance()` inside setup() returns the component instance — but using it for state management is an anti-pattern. Component instances are internal implementation details, not public API. Accessing `instance.ctx` or `instance.data` directly bypasses reactivity tracking. UNSAID: getCurrentInstance() is intended for plugin development, not application code. The correct state access pattern: use the composables and reactive primitives (ref, reactive, computed) that Vue exposes, not the internal component instance. The failure pattern: using getCurrentInstance() to access parent component's state — this creates tight coupling and bypasses Vue's data flow model.

Vue 3 drag-and-drop with Sortable.js integration via composable: wrap Sortable.js initialization in `useSortable(listRef, options)` composable. Initialize Sortable in onMounted, destroy in onBeforeUnmount. Emit sort events to update the parent's reactive state. UNSAID: Vue-Draggable-Plus (modern, Vue 3 compatible) wraps Sortable.js with Vue 3 composables. The key Vue-specific challenge: after a drag operation, the DOM is updated by Sortable.js, but Vue's reactive data hasn't changed — you must listen to Sortable's end event and update the reactive array to match. Failing to sync reactive state with DOM state after drag causes Vue to 'correct' the DOM back to the old order on the next render.

Vue 3 SSR with vue-server-renderer / @vue/server-renderer: `renderToString(app)` generates the HTML string. The `ssrContext` provides request-specific data. Hydration on client: `createSSRApp()` instead of `createApp()`, then `hydrate(app, document.getElementById('app'))`. UNSAID: Vue 3's SSR is more straightforward than React's — no hydration root API difference (createSSRApp vs createApp is the only change). The data fetching for SSR uses onServerPrefetch lifecycle hook: `onServerPrefetch(async () => { await fetchData() })` — called during server rendering to pre-fetch data. The fetched data must be serialized to the SSR context and sent with the HTML for client-side hydration without re-fetching.

Vue 3 memory leaks from unregistered watchers: creating watchers outside of component setup() (in setTimeout callbacks or event handlers) don't auto-cleanup when the component unmounts. Use watchEffect's return value (cleanup function) or call `const stop = watch(...)` and call `stop()` in onBeforeUnmount. UNSAID: watchers created inside setup() are automatically stopped when the component unmounts. Watchers created outside setup() (e.g., in a utility function called from a callback) must be manually stopped. The pattern for safe watcher creation outside setup: check `getCurrentInstance()` — if it returns a value, the watcher can be auto-registered to the component lifecycle; if null, manual cleanup is required.

Vue 3 + Nuxt 3 authentication: nuxt-auth (sidebase/nuxt-auth), nuxt-auth-utils, or custom auth with useCookie for session management. JWT pattern: store JWT in httpOnly cookie (set by server route), read it in middleware to protect routes. `defineNuxtRouteMiddleware((to, from) => { const token = useCookie('token'); if (!token.value) return navigateTo('/login') })`. UNSAID: Nuxt middleware runs on both server and client — server middleware on initial page load, client middleware on navigation. The isomorphic cookie reading (useCookie) works identically on both. nuxt-auth wraps Auth.js (formerly NextAuth) for Nuxt — provides OAuth providers, credentials, and session management. The auth state is accessible via `useAuth()` composable across all components.

Vue 3 Pinia type inference breaks with complex store compositions: when store A uses store B, and TypeScript's circular type inference kicks in, the types may not resolve correctly. UNSAID: the fix is to not circularly depend stores. If store A and B need shared state, extract it to a store C that both A and B use. The Pinia documentation explicitly warns against circular store dependencies. Runtime behavior works (JavaScript modules handle circular dependencies), but TypeScript inference fails. Additionally, Pinia stores' TypeScript types for the setup-style stores are more complex to define than Options-style stores — setup stores have better runtime flexibility but require more TypeScript ceremony.

Vue 3 charts and data visualization: (1) ApexCharts (vue3-apexcharts wrapper), (2) ECharts (vue-echarts wrapper), (3) Chart.js (vue-chartjs wrapper), (4) Vega-Lite for statistical charts. UNSAID: ECharts has the best performance for large datasets (100k+ data points) using Canvas rendering. ApexCharts has the richest SVG-based interactive charts with annotation support. Chart.js is the most widely used globally with extensive plugin ecosystem. For Vue-native integration, vue-chartjs v4+ fully supports Vue 3 and TypeScript. The responsive container pattern: wrap charts in a ResizeObserver and re-render on container size changes — VueUse's `useResizeObserver` composable handles this elegantly.

Vue 3 CI/CD pipeline: (1) `vue-tsc --noEmit` (TypeScript check), (2) `eslint --ext .vue,.ts,.js .` (lint with Vue rules), (3) `vitest run --coverage` (unit tests), (4) `vite build` or `nuxt build` (production build), (5) `playwright test` (E2E tests). UNSAID: `vue-tsc` is the TypeScript type checker for .vue files — it wraps tsc and adds Volar's template type checking. Without vue-tsc in CI, TypeScript errors in templates only surface in the IDE, not the build pipeline. The common CI failure: forgetting to run vue-tsc separately from vite build (Vite builds successfully even with TypeScript errors by default). Add `typescript.tsconfig.strict: true` in Volar settings for maximum strictness in IDE and CI.

Vue 3 ref unwrapping in templates: inside templates, reactive refs are automatically unwrapped — you don't write `count.value`, just `count`. This unwrapping only happens at the top level of the template — nested refs inside objects are not unwrapped. `{{ obj.count.value }}` is still needed if count is a nested ref inside a plain object. UNSAID: automatic unwrapping in templates is a Vue design decision to reduce template boilerplate. The difference between JavaScript (`.value` required) and template (no `.value`) creates mental context switching. The consistent approach: use destructuring with toRefs when multiple refs are used frequently, keeping template access clean. Reactive objects from Pinia storeToRefs() work the same way — destructured refs are auto-unwrapped in templates.

Vue 3 scoped styles specificity: `<style scoped>` adds a unique attribute selector to all rules, increasing specificity. Overriding scoped styles from parent components requires either ::v-deep() (deep selector) or the `!important` hack (never use). Pattern: `:deep(.child-class) { ... }` in parent component reaches into child's scoped styles. UNSAID: overusing :deep() creates implicit coupling between parent and child CSS — the child doesn't control its own styling. The proper pattern for customizable components: CSS custom properties (variables) exposed by the child component, set by the parent. This is an explicit API contract. The scoped attribute is randomly generated at build time — hardcoding `data-v-xxxx` selectors in tests or external CSS is brittle and breaks on rebuild.

Vue 3 composable with asyncState pattern: VueUse's `useAsyncState` wraps async operations with reactive state: `const { state, isLoading, error } = useAsyncState(fetchUser(id), null)`. Automatically handles the loading/error/success lifecycle. UNSAID: useAsyncState is the simplest data fetching pattern in Vue without TanStack Query. It's appropriate for one-time fetches (not recurring queries). For recurring data with caching and background updates, TanStack Vue Query is more appropriate. The resetOnExecute option resets state to the initial value before each execution — prevents showing stale data while loading. Combining useAsyncState with a watch on the id prop enables reactive data fetching when IDs change.

Vue 3 hydration mismatch in Nuxt: using Date.now(), Math.random(), or browser-only APIs during SSR causes hydration mismatches — the server renders one value, the client renders another. The `<ClientOnly>` component wraps content that should only render client-side. UNSAID: `<ClientOnly>` renders nothing on server and only renders its default slot on client after hydration. This fixes hydration mismatches but eliminates the SEO and TTFB benefits for the wrapped content. The fallback slot in `<ClientOnly>` provides server-side placeholder content. For user-specific content (showing logged-in user's name), server-side rendering via cookies (read in server component) avoids the ClientOnly pattern entirely — better UX and SEO.

Vue 3 modal management pattern with Pinia: a modal store manages visibility and payload for modals. `openModal({ type: 'ConfirmDelete', payload: { itemId } })` triggers the modal. A global `ModalContainer` component in the app root renders the active modal. UNSAID: centralized modal management prevents 'modal opening in wrong component' issues and enables stacking modals (confirm inside edit modal). The pattern aligns with React's context-based modal pattern. Alternative: direct boolean refs in each component are simpler but don't scale to complex modal flows. Pinia's modal store enables modal-to-modal navigation (close Modal A, open Modal B) from a single action — impossible with distributed component-level refs.

Vue 2 to Vue 3 migration: (1) Upgrade to Vue 2.7 (backports Composition API for gradual migration), (2) Replace Vuex with Pinia, (3) Replace Vue Router 3 with Vue Router 4, (4) Replace Options API with Composition API component by component, (5) Replace Vue.prototype with provide/inject or composables, (6) Remove filters (use computed or helper functions), (7) Replace v-model argument syntax (v-model:title replaces :title.sync), (8) Upgrade to Vue 3. UNSAID: the @vue/compat compatibility build allows running Vue 3 with Vue 2 compatibility mode — components can be migrated incrementally. The migration from Vuex to Pinia is straightforward: stores map directly (state → setup refs, getters → computed, mutations+actions → actions). Vue Router 4 changes: `mode: 'history'` → `createWebHistory()`, `next()` in guards replaced by return values.

Vue 3.4 performance improvements: reactive destructuring (experimental), faster prop updates, improved compiler output. The reactive destructuring syntax: `const { count, name } = defineProps<Props>()` — props remain reactive (compiler adds effect tracking). Previously, destructuring props lost reactivity. UNSAID: reactive props destructuring requires the `propsDestructureTransform` compiler option. This is a significant DX improvement — previously, accessing `props.count` vs `count` was necessary everywhere. The compiler transforms `const { count } = defineProps()` to track each destructured prop's access individually. Vue 3.4 also improved rendering performance by 40%+ in some benchmarks through optimized prop patching and normalized Virtual DOM operations.

Vue 3 event handling memory leak: registering DOM event listeners with addEventListener in onMounted without removing them in onBeforeUnmount causes leaks for every component mount/unmount cycle in SPAs. UNSAID: VueUse's `useEventListener` composable handles this correctly — it registers and removes event listeners in sync with component lifecycle. Manual addEventListener requires symmetric removeEventListener in cleanup. The leaked listeners accumulate across navigation: a component mounted on every page visit leaves one more listener each time. Long-running SPAs with this pattern see degrading performance as listener count grows. The pattern for window-level event listeners: always use VueUse's useEventListener or implement explicit cleanup.

Vue 3 table component with virtual scrolling: vue-virtual-scroller or @tanstack/vue-virtual. For tables specifically, virtualize the `<tbody>` rows while keeping `<thead>` fixed. The table header position:sticky pattern with virtualized rows requires careful height calculation. UNSAID: virtualizing tables is more complex than lists because of the two-axis layout (headers must align with scrolled content). The simplest approach: use a specialized table library (AG Grid Community for Vue, or naive-ui's DataTable with virtual scroll option) rather than building virtual scroll from scratch. AG Grid's Vue integration provides enterprise-grade features (column pinning, grouping, aggregation) with optional virtual scrolling for million-row datasets.

Vue's 'progressive framework' design: Vue can be used as a script tag (CDN), as a build-tool SPA, as a Nuxt SSR app, or as a Web Component — same core API, increasing capability tiers. This progressive adoption model is Vue's original design philosophy, inspired by how jQuery enabled progressive enhancement before SPAs. UNSAID: the CDN usage pattern (`<script src='vue.global.js'>`, `Vue.createApp()`) still works in Vue 3 and is appropriate for: embedding Vue functionality in server-rendered pages (PHP, Ruby on Rails, Django), admin panels, and marketing sites that want minimal tooling. This use case is underappreciated — Vue is often overlooked for these scenarios where React's JSX requirement makes adoption friction higher.

Nuxt 3 server-side rendering and API routes: the `process.server` guard is required for code that should only run on the server (database access, secret environment variables). Exposing `process.env.DATABASE_URL` in client-side code leaks credentials to the browser. UNSAID: Nuxt 3's `runtimeConfig` distinguishes public (available client-side) and private (server-only) config. `useRuntimeConfig().public.apiBase` is safe to use client-side; `useRuntimeConfig().databaseUrl` is server-only. Environment variables prefixed with `NUXT_PUBLIC_` are automatically exposed; others are server-only. The common mistake: using `process.env.SECRET_KEY` in a component without `process.server` guard — Nuxt bundles this into the client JavaScript during build.

Vue 3 composable testing pattern: test composables in isolation by creating a test component and using Vue Test Utils to mount it. `withSetup(() => useCounter())` helper mounts a test component and captures the composable's return value. UNSAID: composables can't be called outside of a component's setup context — they need an active Vue component instance for lifecycle hooks and provide/inject. The withSetup pattern wraps the composable in a minimal component for testing. For composables that don't use lifecycle hooks or provide/inject, they can be called and tested directly without a component wrapper. VueUse provides a `makeDestructurable` utility that makes composable return values work with both destructuring and direct access.

Vue 3 animation with GSAP: GSAP works with Vue via refs and lifecycle hooks. `onMounted(() => { gsap.to(el.value, { x: 100, duration: 1 }) })`. For enter/leave animations, use Vue's Transition component with JavaScript hooks (onEnter, onLeave, onAfterLeave). UNSAID: GSAP's Vue plugin doesn't exist — integration is via refs and lifecycle hooks. The pattern: ref the target element, animate in onMounted, reverse in onBeforeUnmount. Using GSAP's gsap.context(cleanup, el) scopes all GSAP animations to a container and enables one-line cleanup — `return () => ctx.revert()` in onBeforeUnmount. The combination of Vue Transition hooks + GSAP timeline gives fine-grained control over complex animation sequences.

Vue 3 v-for key reactivity: keys must be unique and stable. Using index as key causes the same reconciliation bugs as React — wrong component reuse on insert/delete. Keys must come from stable data IDs. UNSAID: Vue's reconciliation with keys is identical to React's — the key identifies which virtual DOM node maps to which real DOM node. Without keys, Vue uses in-place patch strategy (updates the content of existing elements) — correct for simple lists, but wrong for stateful elements (inputs, components with internal state). Adding :key forces Vue to track identity explicitly. The debugging symptom of wrong keys: input values appearing in the wrong rows after filtering or sorting operations.

Vue 3 server components (Nuxt Island): `<NuxtIsland>` renders a component entirely on the server — zero client JavaScript for the island. The component must be named with `.server.vue` suffix. Multiple islands can be on the same page with mixed client/server rendering. UNSAID: Nuxt Islands are Vue's equivalent of React Server Components — server-rendered component fragments with no client JavaScript. Islands are ideal for: complex server-fetched content (product catalogs, article content), components that don't need interactivity, and parts of the page where JavaScript is wasted. The island architecture enables partial hydration — only interactive parts of the page load JavaScript, dramatically reducing bundle size for content-heavy pages.

Vue 3 performance audit checklist: (1) Check Vue DevTools Profiler for slow component renders, (2) Look for unnecessary computed property recalculations, (3) Check v-for lists for missing keys and unnecessary renders, (4) Identify large component trees that could use v-memo, (5) Check for synchronous watchers causing render cascades, (6) Measure bundle size with rollup-plugin-visualizer, (7) Add defineAsyncComponent for heavy components, (8) Enable code splitting at route level. UNSAID: Vue 3's compiler static analysis (static hoisting, patch flags) handles most performance automatically. Manual optimization is rarely needed for typical business apps — the need for manual optimization indicates either a data structure problem or an algorithmic issue that optimization won't fix.

Vue 3's defineComponent() is optional for Composition API but required for Options API to get TypeScript inference. With `<script setup>`, defineComponent is unnecessary — TypeScript inference works without it. The function exists primarily for: (1) TypeScript inference in Options API, (2) Explicit component typing in render functions, (3) Legacy Vue 2 compatibility. UNSAID: the confusion between `defineComponent()` and `<script setup>` comes from learning resources that mix API styles. For new Vue 3 projects, `<script setup lang='ts'>` is the recommended approach — no defineComponent() needed. defineComponent() is important for: component libraries that need to support both script setup and Options API consumers, and render-function-based components.

Vue 3 reactive() and Array.from(): `Array.from(reactiveArray)` returns a non-reactive copy of the array. This is correct for creating snapshot copies but wrong if you need to maintain reactivity. UNSAID: methods that create new arrays from reactive arrays always produce non-reactive plain arrays. The reactive context is only maintained through direct property access on the reactive Proxy. When passing reactive arrays to third-party functions that return new arrays, the returned arrays won't be reactive — you must re-wrap them: `reactive(thirdPartyFn(reactiveArray))`. This is rarely a problem in practice because most array operations in Vue should be done through computed properties (which maintain reactivity) rather than imperative transformations.

Nuxt 3 SEO: `useSeoMeta()` composable sets meta tags with full TypeScript support for all OpenGraph, Twitter Card, and standard meta properties. `useHead()` for more complex cases including script injection. UNSAID: useSeoMeta is TypeScript-aware — it knows all valid Open Graph property names and their value types, catching typos at compile time. The `useServerSeoMeta()` variant only runs on the server (smaller client bundle). Nuxt SEO module bundles useSeoMeta, robots.txt, sitemap.xml, and canonical URL generation — use it for content-heavy sites. The `defineOgImageComponent()` generates Open Graph images from Vue components using Satori (render Vue templates to PNG server-side).

Vue 3 Devtools Vue Plugin protocol: build custom DevTools panels using the Vue Plugin API exposed by Vue DevTools. Useful for: custom state visualization, component metrics, application-specific debugging panels. UNSAID: most production applications don't need custom DevTools panels — the built-in component inspector, Pinia panel, and Vue Router panel cover 95% of debugging needs. Custom panels are appropriate for: complex reactive state systems, application-specific metrics (API call timing, cache hit rates), and framework-level debugging in complex setups. The DevTools plugin API is documented but stable — it won't change between minor Vue DevTools versions.

Vue 3 setup() timing: setup() runs after props resolution but before component mounting. onMounted callback runs after the component is mounted to the DOM. Accessing template refs ($refs) inside setup() returns null — the DOM doesn't exist yet. UNSAID: this is one of the most common Vue 3 confusion points: `const el = ref(null); console.log(el.value)` in setup() prints null. The ref is populated after mounting. The correct access point is onMounted(). The timing sequence: props resolved → setup() runs → beforeMount lifecycle → component renders → DOM created → mounted lifecycle. Template refs are populated during the 'component renders' step, making them available in onMounted but not in setup().

Vue 3 plugin development: `install(app, options)` function. `app.component()` registers global components, `app.directive()` registers global directives, `app.provide()` provides plugin state to all components. UNSAID: Vue plugins are objects with an install method (or functions that act as install). The install function receives the app instance — this is the only time to register global behaviors. Plugin options are passed via `app.use(plugin, options)`. Distributing a Vue 3 plugin as an npm package requires exporting the plugin object, and optionally TypeScript types for the provide/inject contract. Vue 3 plugins work identically in CDN usage (global `app.use()`) and bundler usage — the install API is stable.

Vue 3 Nuxt 3 environment variables: `process.env.SECRET` is only available server-side. Client-side access requires adding the variable to `runtimeConfig.public` in nuxt.config.ts AND using `NUXT_PUBLIC_` prefix in .env. Non-prefixed env vars are server-only even if they're in runtimeConfig.public. UNSAID: the Nuxt 3 env var system has three tiers: (1) Server-only: standard .env vars, never exposed to client, (2) Public: NUXT_PUBLIC_ prefix in .env or runtimeConfig.public in config, available in both, (3) Build-time: accessed during build, baked into the bundle. Accessing a server-only variable in a client component causes an undefined value at runtime — no error, just undefined. The Nuxt DevTools environment tab shows the current effective runtime config, helpful for debugging missing variables.

Vue 3 code review checklist: (1) Props declared and typed, (2) Emits declared with types, (3) No direct prop mutation, (4) Watchers have cleanup (deep watchers especially), (5) No side effects in computed properties, (6) Keys present in all v-for, (7) Template refs accessed only in onMounted+, (8) Composables called inside setup() only, (9) Reactive state not destructured without toRefs/storeToRefs, (10) No direct reactive()/ref() outside of setup or composables. UNSAID: points 1-3 are the most commonly violated in team settings. Props without types cause runtime surprises; undeclared emits break IDE autocomplete for parent consumers; prop mutation creates one-way data flow violations. The checklist should be enforced via ESLint rules (vue/require-prop-types, vue/require-explicit-emits, vue/no-mutating-props).

Vue 3 vs React 18 decision framework: choose Vue 3 when: server-side rendering with Nuxt is needed, team is more comfortable with HTML-like templates, progressive enhancement in server-rendered apps is required, or design system uses Vuetify/PrimeVue. Choose React when: team is large with varied React expertise, ecosystem breadth is critical (most UI libraries have React-first support), or Next.js features (Vercel deployment, React Server Components) are needed. UNSAID: the choice is rarely technical — it's team expertise and ecosystem fit. Both Vue 3 and React 18 are excellent production choices. The real differentiator in 2024: Meta/Vercel backing React's SSR future vs. Evan You/community backing Vue's Vapor mode future. For international markets (China, Southeast Asia), Vue has stronger ecosystem and community.

Vue 3 scoped slot with dynamic component: using scoped slots with `<component :is='dynamicComp'>` requires the dynamic component to properly define the slot contract in its template. If the dynamic component doesn't expose the slot, the slot content is silently dropped. UNSAID: this is a Vue-specific gotcha — TypeScript won't catch mismatched slot contracts between the dynamic component and the parent's slot template. The fix: use TypeScript generic slot typing (available in Vue 3.3+ with `<script setup generic='...'>`), or avoid dynamic components with complex slot APIs. The debugging approach: temporarily replace the dynamic component with a static known-good component to isolate whether the issue is the dynamic dispatch or the slot contract.

Vue 3 with Stripe Elements: use `@stripe/stripe-js` to load Stripe, mount Elements to a div ref in onMounted, unmount in onBeforeUnmount. `stripe.confirmPayment()` with the Elements instance. UNSAID: Vue's ref system handles the DOM element lifecycle correctly for Stripe Elements integration. The key pattern: `const stripeElements = ref(null)` — assign in onMounted after stripe.elements() call, call stripeElements.value.destroy() in onBeforeUnmount. The CardElement's onChange event updates validation state reactively. Composition API makes Stripe integration cleaner than Options API — the entire Stripe setup (stripe instance, elements, card element, confirmation logic) fits cleanly in a single useStripe() composable.

Vue 3 infinite scroll with Intersection Observer: `useIntersectionObserver` from VueUse observes when a sentinel element enters the viewport, triggering next page load. Combined with TanStack Vue Query's `useInfiniteQuery`, this creates a complete infinite scroll solution. UNSAID: the sentinel element must be positioned after the last item in the list. When it becomes visible, the observer callback fires and `fetchNextPage()` is called. VueUse's `useIntersectionObserver` returns a `stop` function — call it when no more pages are available (`!hasNextPage`) to disconnect the observer and prevent unnecessary intersection checks. The `rootMargin` option extends the observable area, triggering fetch before the sentinel is fully visible — pre-fetching for seamless scrolling.

Vue 3 TypeScript infer-component-emit-types: when consuming a component via template, parent TypeScript doesn't validate emit payloads by default — it's a Volar feature that must be enabled. Without proper Volar setup, passing wrong handler signatures to @emit doesn't cause TypeScript errors.
